raw_response_text:
<features>
- petal color: deep pink/magenta
- petal shape: large, rounded, reflexed (curving backwards)
- flower structure: cup-like or bell-like shape with petals pointing upwards/backwards
- leaf appearance: dark green, broad leaves visible in background
</features>

<kb>
- IF [petal color = pink/magenta AND petal shape = reflexed/upward-pointing] THEN [87]
- IF [petal color = pink/purple AND flower structure = spike-like/conical with small side flowers] THEN [92]
- IF [petal color = yellow AND petal shape = large, flat, spreading petals] THEN [14]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- The rule for class 87 fires because the Query Image shows large, deep pink petals that are reflexed (curving upwards and backwards), which matches the pattern seen in the examples for class 87.
</rule_check>

<response>87</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

<features>
- petal color: deep pink/magenta

raw_response_text:
<features>
- central structure: large, pink, cone-shaped/spiky bracts
- side flowers: small, purple, three-petaled flowers
- background: dark/black
</features>

<kb>
- IF [flower has large pink cone-shaped bracts AND small purple side flowers] THEN [92]
- IF [flower has large, upright, pink/purple petals with a darker base] THEN [87]
- IF [flower is yellow with distinct petal patterns] THEN [14]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- The rule for class 92 fires because the image contains a large, pink, spiky central structure (bracts) accompanied by small purple flowers on the side, which matches the pattern seen in the support images for class 92.
</rule_check>

<response>92</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

raw_response_text:
<features>
- flower color: yellow
- petal pattern: dark lines/veins on the lower petals
- petal shape: large, broad, drooping petals
</features>
<kb>
- IF [flower color = yellow AND petal pattern = dark lines/veins] THEN [14]
- IF [flower color = pink/purple AND petal shape = upright/reflexed] THEN [87]
- IF [flower color = pink/purple AND structure = cone-like/spike with small flowers] THEN [92]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- The rule for class 14 fires because the Query Image shows a yellow flower with distinct dark lines/veins on its broad, drooping petals, matching the pattern seen in the examples for class 14.
</rule_check>

<response>14</response>
